Rule_Text: All clocks and resets must be documented in TOP_HEADER section with: (a) clock name and frequency, (b) clock domain, (c) clock source (primary/generated/derived), (d) reset name and polarity (active-high/active-low), (e) reset synchronization strategy, (f) clock-to-clock relationships for multi-clock designs. This information must be extractable for synthesis constraint generation.
All clock signals must be clearly identified with '_clk' or 'clk_' naming convention; document clock frequency and duty cycle requirements in TOP_HEADER.
All reset signals must be clearly identified with '_rst' or '_rst_n' or 'rst_' naming convention; document reset polarity (active-high or active-low) in TOP_HEADER.
Generated clocks must be properly constrained in timing constraints file; document generated clock derivation, frequency, phase relationship to source clock, and generation logic.
Clock groups must be defined for asynchronous clock domains; document which clocks are async with rationale and CDC strategy.
All clock gating logic must be clearly identified with section header and follow project-specific gating methodology; use 'STA_CG' tag in comment to mark clock gating cells for STA tool recognition.
Clock gating cells must use integrated clock gating (ICG) cells provided by library; do not hand-instantiate gates for clock gating; document gating conditions and power savings rationale.
Clock tree insertion: high-fanout clocks should use balanced clock tree; document expected clock skew, insertion delay, and jitter requirements in TOP_HEADER.
Clock domain crossing (CDC) synthesis: use only proven synchronizer patterns; document which CDC synchronizer pattern used (2FF, MCP FIFO, handshake, Gray-code).
For DFT (Design For Test), avoid gated clocks in scan chains unless using test-mode bypass; document DFT strategy, scan insertion points, and clock gating test mode.
Reset synchronization: async reset de-assertion must be synchronized to destination clock domain; use reset synchronizer circuit with at least 2 flip-flops.
Document reset initialization strategy: whether design relies on explicit reset or power-on-reset (POR); list all state elements that must be reset vs those that can power up to X.
Reset distribution: for large designs, use reset tree with documented fanout limits per reset buffer stage; document reset assertion and de-assertion timing.
Document reset scope: global reset vs local resets; if using local resets, document reset domain boundaries and reset sequencing requirements.
Async reset recovery and removal timing must be met; document recovery/removal timing constraints and any special reset synchronization requirements.
Two-flop synchronizers must be used for all single-bit signals crossing async clock domains; mark synchronizer chain to prevent optimization with 'ASYNC_REG' or synthesis-tool-specific attribute.
Multi-bit CDC must never use simple 2FF synchronizers; use Gray-code, MCP FIFO, handshake, or other safe multi-bit CDC technique; document CDC technique choice and rationale.
Synchronizer chain registers must be physically placed close together; use synthesis constraints or attributes to prevent spreading; document placement constraints.
Metastability: all CDC paths must have sufficient MTBF (mean time between failures) for application; document MTBF calculation assumptions, clock frequencies, and reliability requirements.
All CDC paths must be identified in TOP_HEADER with: (a) source clock domain, (b) destination clock domain, (c) CDC technique used, (d) signal name(s) crossing, (e) whether data or control signal.
CDC assertion synchronizers must include convergence logic to ensure sampling stability; document convergence requirements and sampling strategy.
For FIFO-based CDC, document FIFO depth calculation showing gray-code pointer synchronization latency and data throughput requirements.
Critical timing paths exceeding project-specific threshold of logic levels (prompt user for threshold, default N=5) must be documented and justified; use 'STA_CRITICAL' tag in comment.
All outputs of combinatorial logic blocks must have clear fanout destinations documented if unusual fanout occurs; use 'STA_CRITICAL' tag in comment for timing-critical fanout paths.
For high-fanout signals (>100 destinations, prompt user for threshold), document fanout explicitly and consider adding pipeline stage or buffer tree replication for timing closure.
Minimize logic depth between register stages; target maximum N logic levels (prompt user, default 3-5 for high-frequency designs, 5-8 for moderate frequency).
False paths must be documented in design and constrained in SDC/XDC files; reference specific source and destination registers and reason for false path declaration.
Multi-cycle paths must be documented with timing rationale; ensure multi-cycle constraints match actual design behavior and document why multiple cycles are safe.
Datapath balancing: ensure pipeline stages have roughly equal logic depth for optimal frequency; document stage-by-stage logic depth and critical path within each stage.
Critical timing paths: identify and document top 10 critical paths in design; ensure these paths have design attention and potential optimization strategies listed.
Setup and hold timing: verify both setup and hold timing close; document hold-fixing strategy (buffers, delays, useful skew) if needed.
Clock-to-Q delays: account for register clock-to-Q delays and clock skew in timing budgets; document timing budgets for module interfaces and handshake signals.
Combinatorial output paths: avoid when possible as they create difficult timing constraints; if necessary, document required external input-to-output timing and provide timing budget.
Use register output timing: prefer registering outputs at module boundaries; document any combinatorial outputs with justification and external timing requirements.
Input-to-register timing: document input setup/hold requirements relative to clock; provide timing budgets for external devices driving inputs.

Evaluate design and report suspected long combinatorial paths that may not meet static timing; prompt user for target clock frequency if not defined to estimate setup failure risk; use 'TODO: STA_FAILURE' tag in comment for these paths to indicate they must be reviewed and addressed.
For timing closure, identify register insertion opportunities: places where adding pipeline stage would break critical path with acceptable latency increase.
Document timing exceptions comprehensively: for each false path or multi-cycle path, explain functional reason why exception is safe.

Resource-intensive constructs (multipliers, large muxes, memories, complex arithmetic) must be documented with estimated area/timing impact; use 'STA_CRITICAL' tag in comment for timing-critical resources.

No inferred memories allowed unless explicitly approved; only use explicitly instantiated memory macros and be consistent within module; prefer interface to externally instantiated and accessible memory for better control and area optimization.
For RAMs, ROMs, DSP blocks, explicitly instantiate vendor primitives when necessary for area/performance; document primitive usage, configuration, and memory layout.
For FPGA targets, use BRAM inference patterns or explicit instantiation; document memory mapping strategy (distributed RAM vs BRAM vs URAM) with selection rationale.
For ASIC targets, use memory compiler instances; document memory configuration (words, width, banks, ports, mux ratio) and access timing (setup/hold, access time).
Document memory partitioning strategy: single large memory vs multiple smaller banks; explain tradeoffs (area, timing, power, access patterns).
For arithmetic operations, use appropriate bit widths; avoid unnecessary wide arithmetic that wastes area; document width determination rationale and overflow handling.
Pipelining arithmetic: complex arithmetic (multiply, divide, square root) should be pipelined; document pipeline depth, latency, and throughput in TOP_HEADER.
Resource sharing: identify opportunities where arithmetic units can be time-multiplexed; document sharing strategy and area/timing tradeoffs with control logic overhead.
For multipliers, document whether inferring (*) operator or using explicit DSP primitive; justify choice based on size, performance, and resource availability.
Large mux structures should be balanced; avoid highly unbalanced if-else chains that create long combinatorial paths; use case statements for one-hot or binary-encoded selection.
Document resource utilization estimates in TOP_HEADER: registers, LUTs/gates, RAMs, DSPs; provide percentage of target device if known.
One-hot state machines require safe state encoding: include 'unique' or 'priority' keywords and consider fallback logic for safety-critical designs to handle illegal states.
FSM encoding: allow synthesis tool to choose encoding unless specific encoding required (one-hot, binary, Gray); document if manual encoding used and why (power, area, timing).
FSM state reachability: document all states and verify all are reachable; unreachable states indicate dead logic or missing functionality.
FSM output logic: prefer registered outputs (Moore) over combinatorial (Mealy) for timing closure; document if Mealy used and timing rationale.
FSM state vector: use enumerated types for state definitions; document state encoding in TOP_HEADER if manually specified.
FSM error states: include error/illegal state handling; document recovery mechanism or safe state on error detection.
Avoid combinatorial loops which prevent synthesis and cause X propagation; if unavoidable for specific protocol (e.g., async handshake), document extensively with justification and mitigation.
Latch inference is almost always unintentional; all combinatorial always_comb/always@(*) blocks must assign all outputs in all branches to prevent latch inference.
Use SystemVerilog always_comb for combinatorial logic to enable additional compile-time checks; avoid always@(*) style.

Case statement intent: always use standard 'case' with a mandatory 'default' branch; do NOT use 'unique case' or 'priority case' (prohibited by VERILOG_FSM,3); avoid 'full_case parallel_case' pragmas.

Default case statements: always include a 'default' branch in every case statement; do NOT rely on 'unique' or 'priority' keywords as substitutes for an explicit default (see VERILOG_FSM,3); missing default cases can cause latch inference or X propagation.
X-propagation: ensure combinatorial logic propagates X values correctly for simulation; avoid structures that mask X values hiding initialization issues.
Pipeline stage insertion points must be clearly marked with comments indicating stage number and data latency from input to that stage.
For deep pipelines (>5 stages), document pipeline control signals: valid/enable, stall, flush, and their propagation through pipeline.
Pipeline balance: all pipeline stages should have similar logic depth; document logic depth per stage and identify any unbalanced stages.
Pipeline retiming: document whether design allows synthesis tool retiming across pipeline; use retiming attributes if supported.
Retiming opportunities: place register stages at module boundaries to allow synthesis tools to retime across modules if boundary registering enabled.
Bubble propagation: for stall-able pipelines, document bubble handling and verify bubbles don't cause control logic issues.
Pipeline flush: document flush mechanism and verify all pipeline stages clear correctly on flush; consider side-effects on counters/state.
Pipeline depth parameter: make pipeline depth parameterizable when feasible; document tradeoffs between latency and frequency.
Constant propagation: use parameters and localparams effectively so synthesis tools can optimize for specific parameter values and prune unused logic.
Boundary optimization: control whether synthesis can optimize across module boundaries; document optimization boundaries with hierarchy preservation pragmas if needed.
Dont-touch directives: use to preserve certain logic structures for debugging or specific implementation; document all dont-touch regions with reason.
Use synthesis directives sparingly; prefer portable RTL over tool-specific pragmas; document all synthesis directives with justification and alternative if porting.
Size and timing constraints: provide synthesis tool with realistic constraints; over-constraining wastes area/power, under-constraining creates timing failures.
Document synthesis constraints template in TOP_HEADER: expected SDC/XDC constraint commands for clocks, I/O timing, false paths, multi-cycle paths.
For phase-locked loops (PLLs) and clock generation, provide jitter specifications and synthesized clock constraints in comments.
Post-synthesis verification: document requirement for gate-level simulation and specific scenarios to test (timing, X-propagation, CDC).
Equivalence checking: document whether formal equivalence checking is required between RTL and netlist; list any expected differences (black-boxes, tech cells).
Timing simulation: document requirement for back-annotated SDF simulation; list critical test cases requiring gate-level timing verification.
X-propagation analysis: document strategy for X-initialization; whether design is X-tolerant or requires explicit initialization.
Power analysis: document dynamic and static power estimates; identify high-power blocks and optimization strategies applied (clock gating, operand isolation).
Area analysis: document area breakdown by function; identify area-critical blocks and optimization strategies applied.
Power optimization: identify signals with high toggle rate and low criticality; consider operand isolation, clock gating, or power gating.
Clock gating efficiency: document percentage of design clock-gated; identify high-activity blocks as gating candidates.
Operand isolation: for arithmetic units, gate operands when result unused; document isolation strategy and power savings.
Power domains: for multi-voltage designs, document power domain boundaries, isolation cells, level shifters, and power sequence.
Toggle rate analysis: document high-toggle signals and whether toggles are functional or wasteful; optimize control logic to reduce wasteful transitions.
For area optimization, identify non-critical paths that can tolerate slower logic; use synthesis constraints to deprioritize timing on these paths for area recovery.
Compile strategy: document synthesis compile options (effort level, area vs speed optimization, gate-level optimization phases).
Use incremental synthesis for large designs; document module compilation order and synthesis partitioning strategy.
Resource allocation: document strategy for FSM encoding, arithmetic implementation, memory selection based on design goals (speed, area, power).
Ungrouping hierarchy: document which module boundaries can be ungrouped for optimization and which must be preserved for design intent.
Design structure: organize logic into modules with clear boundaries; avoid mixing protocol control with datapath logic for better synthesis QOR.
Interface timing budgets: document setup/hold requirements and clock-to-Q delays for all module interfaces; provide timing budgets for integration.
Registered interfaces: strongly prefer registered inputs and outputs at module boundaries; document any combinatorial pass-through with timing budget.
For high-speed serial interfaces: use dedicated SERDES primitives; document serialization ratio, bit rate, clocking strategy, and equalization settings.
Synchronous interfaces: document relationship between interface clock and module clocks; specify setup/hold relative to interface clock edge.
Asynchronous interfaces: document all async interface signals and their synchronization strategy; mark async inputs clearly in TOP_HEADER.
Back-pressure and flow control: document handshake protocol timing; ensure ready/valid or credit-based flow control meets timing without combinatorial loops.
Parameterization: make designs parameterizable (data widths, depths, counts); document parameter valid ranges and inter-parameter constraints.
Naming conventions: use consistent naming for clocks (_clk), resets (_rst_n), active-low signals (_n), registered outputs (_r or _q).
Comments for synthesis: add comments explaining design intent where synthesis choices are critical; mark timing-critical paths, resource-specific implementations.
Code style: use non-blocking assignments (<=) for sequential logic, blocking assignments (=) for combinatorial logic; never mix in same always block.
Avoid vendor-specific constructs unless necessary; document portability limitations when using FPGA/ASIC-specific primitives.
File organization: separate datapath from control logic; use clear module partitioning; document module hierarchy and dataflow in TOP_HEADER.
Technology independence: write RTL that synthesizes across technologies; use technology mapping for primitives; document technology-dependent sections.
Synthesis simulation mismatch: avoid RTL constructs that simulate differently than they synthesize; test synthesis netlist in simulation.
Documentation in TOP_HEADER must include synthesis guidelines: target frequency, critical paths, resource expectations, timing methodology.
For timing closure, maintain at least 5-10% timing margin after place-and-route; document slack targets and timing methodology in TOP_HEADER.
